questions.prompt: Which relative pronoun is used to refer to people in a defining relative clause?
questions.explanation: "Who" refers to people in a relative clause: "The teacher who inspired me retired last year." "Which" refers to things or animals.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative form of the adjective "happy"?
questions.explanation: Short adjectives ending in consonant + -y change the y to i and add -er: happy → happier (than). "more happy" is wrong because "more" is only for long adjectives; "happyer" keeps the y instead of changing it to i; "the happiest" is the superlative, not the comparative.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the superlative form?

options.text: This is more difficult question in the exam.
This is most difficult question in the exam.
This is the most difficult question in the exam.
This is difficultest question in the exam.

questions.explanation: Superlatives always require the definite article "the" before them: the most difficult. Long adjectives use "the most + adjective".
"Whose" replaces a possessive: "The girl whose bag was lost" means the girl's bag was lost. It refers to people.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly expresses equality using "as ... as"?

options.text: Sami is as taller as his brother.
Sami is as tall as his brother.
Sami is more tall as his brother.
Sami is tallest as his brother.

questions.explanation: The structure for equality is "as + adjective (base form) + as". Never use the comparative form inside this structure: "as tall as", not "as taller as".
